So just general info on Deepgram to kick things off.
So Deepgram is a foundational AI company.
What really that means is we're really heavily research and engineering-led.
Like, we have a huge research team, and what we do is we build these voice models for voice AI.
kind crowd like right?
Customers and stuff, and oftentimes what happens is research will build a model, and they'll do research and solve these difficult problems, and then product will productize those models.
But that's sort of like our core DNA is being this sort of research and engineering-led company.
The founders, they were particle of matter physicists who spit signals into the middle of the earth to do some research.
And then those were like sound waves and stuff.
So they basically used that then to build like a speech text like model using end-to deep learning.
So that just gives a background of like where we're coming from with this stuff.
Okay, so just quick narrative.
So voice is sort of like this untapped resource within companies, within businesses, within enterprises.
There's all these voice interactions going on on a regular basis.
All these customer calls, all these like sales calls, business meetings, and it's sort of like stuck in silos.
It's sort of this untapped resource.
So when you think about it, there's different use cases and different ways to use the voice, all this voice data on all these sort of like millions and billions of interactions.

So you could build voice agents, you can build agent assists, where you have an agent that basically can summarize a call or like listening in a call and then give a customer support or salesperson real-time assistance on what they should say and stuff like that.
There's voice AI bots and co-pilots.
There's like, if you think about a drive-thru, you can basically automate that and save on the human labor costs by building agents to manage a drive-thru and order-taking and stuff.
And then like medical dictation, note-taking, that type of stuff as well.
So all these are just examples of like ways to leverage all that untapped data for the voice data that exists.
And the key thing is like text is really different than voice.
So that's something that we...
So when they're looking into, like, just AI in general, and a lot of AI is text-based LLMs and stuff like that, a voice is its own sort of animal and presents a different set of challenges.
So we hear that validation from our customers.
Voice is different than text.

And the thing about perception is, it's like when you think about, like, a voice pipeline, so even think about, like, human-to-human interaction, the first thing you do is listen and understand and process what someone's saying before you respond, right?
So perception is really hard, and it's sort of this garbage-in, garbage-out idea.
So if you don't have the proper perception and proper inputs coming into the voice model, then what you're spitting out or responding with is not going to make any sense at all.

And there is this sort of, like, voice AI maturity curve that we see companies going on this journey or even the market going on this journey where, you know, voice is something that companies already use.

A lot of, as far as like in their customer support, they have these legacy, you know, phone trees and legacy interactive voice response systems, you know, press zero to talk to an operator, press one for English, press two for Spanish, all this stuff.
You get stuck in this phone tree.
So that's basically the legacy option that exists today.
They're starting to move into this automated speech-to-text and text-to-speech.
That's still sort of like still aeroplane or robotic, robotic, especially if it's like not a great experience.
But then now we're in sort of like these phases three, four, and hopefully five in the future where it's like becoming very human, like very natural with these voice bots.
And then eventually we're getting to these voice AI agents.
This year it's a huge thing around creating voice agents and then agentic AI in the future.
And what makes voice AI so hard and what's it, how is it different than text?
So when you think about all the things that human can do in, you know, conversation or interaction, they can multitask, they can process multiple types of information.
They have a contextual understanding of the conversation.
They can comprehend and understand what's being said with the nuance, with understanding emotional pacing, cadence, emotional tone, and stuff like that.
Obviously, he's responding quickly.
If an agent doesn't respond quickly, it's going to throw everything off, even if it sounds natural.
And then humans can take turns.
I recognize when you're about to interrupt me or you have something to say, I'll stop and listen.
And then, obviously, humans can express emotion in the way they talk and then take action from what is heard and what they say.
So those are some of the challenging things.
So we're trying to approximate the human experience, and that's what voice AI is trying to do.
But where most systems fall short is basically in all these things.
All these things are ways that existing voice AI systems are very much challenged.
And we're trying to approximate that human interaction, but they haven't.

We tend to focus on these sort of enterprise use cases, which is more around like customer support.
Or medical triage or stuff like that.

And then we're also building customizing models for our customers.

We can curate data sources, and we can also do things like hot swapping models without affecting latency and stuff like that.

Another important piece of differentiation is we have flexible deployment.
So we can do things like host models on our customer's infrastructure.

We can self-host the models.

Okay, speech-to-speech.
So we have basically a voice agent API, and I'll of explain what this is.
So you would think it's like, okay, to build a voice agent, you have three main components.
You have speech-to-text on the front end.

You have an LLM in the middle that kind of takes the transcript and then thinks about the response, and then it spits out, it gives that response to a text-to-speech model, and then you hear the response.
So you would think that's the sort of model, this sort of like pipeline that comes in, and that's all you need.
But in reality, that's not all you need.
Okay.
don't
A lot of orchestration that goes on around all that.
A lot of developers, they think they're just going to do DIY and just kind of hook everything up together and it's all going to work out.
But that's really not how it works.
You have to manage things like taking turns, manage things like interruptions.
You have to build sort of this conversational logic.
What if you want the LLM to do a lookup on some kind of function or look up some data from some other data source and then respond?
So there's a lot that goes into this.
This green box sort of represents sort of where our voice agent API lives.
So if you think about the user, they're going to usually call it, use some kind of telephony system like a Twilio or something like that, that handles sort of the call logic.
Then they're going to pass the audio data to us, which is our Nova 3 speech-to-text model.
We have sort of this end-of-thought voice activity detection as a part of that.
Again, this is not unique to us.
Any kind of voice pipeline is going to have some of this stuff.
This
Like a turn-taking model, end of thought, detecting end of thought, detecting if a user stops speaking.
If the user stops speaking, or sorry, if it's constantly listening, so let's say we're spitting back out a response, it's constantly listening.
If it hears the user start speaking, it has to basically pause the voice and say, okay, we need to start listening now.
So this sort of like voice activity detection, if it detects an interruption, it'll then tell our text-to-speech to basically pause or stop the response.

But you take the text, you take the audio, you produce a transcript from it, and then from there, then you have to start like working with the LLM to actually think about what it is that the user is asking for.
There are things like establishing context, configuration, conversation history, because with each turn, you want to remember what was happening with the previous response.
Then you basically pass all that information to an LLM.
It's going to do some processing.
Maybe it's going to require looking at RAG, looking at proprietary data, and then finally spitting back out a response.
But there's all these other features as well.
How do you update the system mid-conversation?
How do you update the prompt?
Let's say based on the transcript, you want it to be a different type of agent.
How do you update that?
And then this thing called function calling.
This is where you do lookups to external systems.
Let's say you want to look up the weather, or you want to look up some kind of knowledge retrieval or something like that.
You do something called function calling.
Again, all this stuff is very complicated to kind of code together.
And when you think about it, if you have all of this separate, it adds latency between each hop or each step in the process.
So this is where having us take care of all that in sort of the single unified interface.
It simplifies the developer experience.
It optimizes latency.
It does a lot of things.

We created this benchmark called VACI that measures the things that are important to voice agents.
So latency is obviously an important one.
But one of the important things is, like, you don't want your agent to interrupt you to, like, in.
If you use a filler word, like, um, or ah, or as you're thinking or as you're looking up your order ID, you don't want the agent to think you're done talking.
So you don't want them to falsely interrupt you.
At the same time, you don't want them to wait too long before they start speaking.
Otherwise they...

I'll just give you a quick timeline here.
We launched our text-to-speech.
We launched a text-to-speech, the first version of our Aura model, which was Aura 1.
We actually launched that last year, but it was not very good.
So nobody used it at all.
We launched Aura 2, which is competitive with some of the other players out there, in April, April 15th.
We have started to get deals there, but still, it's like, that's just a new business that's growing.
And then our voice agent API, we just launched that in June for in-production use cases.
So those are two just very new use cases.
We've started to get more deals on them.
But
That's like all of our content, most of our content and stuff is around speech-to-text.
Yep.
Yeah.
Gotcha.
And I assume our kind of content going forward outside of like looking at 11 Labs for kind of ideas, inspiration, et cetera.
You guys want a more holistic kind of voice AI approach so that text-to-speech, speech-to-text, voice API, well-rounded.
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah, for sure.
Key, like voice agents are a very hot topic right now.
So there's lots of content and stuff being a producer on that.
And I think the benefit of voice agents is like it's inclusive of speech-to-text and text-to-speech.
Whenever you talk about voice agents, you have to talk about the other products.
So kind of leading with that, that's important.
But at the same time, each text-to-speech has its own unique topics and stuff like that.
And so does speech-to-text.
Yep.
Yeah.
just saw.
Yeah, they just made an announcement on there.
Yeah, they're one of the main competitors for us on all three, really.
But yeah, VoiceAgent API, they're very similar to us.
But you guys beat them on price on every API.
Yes.
Well, I don't know so much about the other API.
I like speech-to-text and text-to-speech, but definitely on the VoiceAgent API.

If you look at our VoiceAgent blog, there's a deep dive section on pricing, where we actually reference a third-party blog that talks about their pricing model and how convoluted it is and how it can get out of hand, especially when you add, like, you think about, like, a customer support call that, like, grows in context and, like, a long call.
It gets completely out of hand.
And every single time...
Every every single time, you're having to analyze the full context history, and that's how the rising gets out of hand.
Yeah.
I was looking at it yesterday.
They do it by token limit, not by hour.
And so it's just like, okay, well, it's the translation between tokens and how long the call is going to be.
Yeah.
So developers can't, they don't really know how to predict how much it's going to cost because it is token.
And then it's like tokens, like audio tokens are different than text tokens, right?
How do you measure an audio token?
That's not clear.
That's not clearly documented anywhere.
So if you look at their forums and stuff like that, there's a lot of people like complain about their cost models and stuff.
That's awesome.
Well, this is super efficient to make it through all that content.
Anything else that we didn't talk about that you guys want to just mention as part of kind of the ongoing content strategy?
Anything we didn't touch on today?
Yeah, I think Pippin, she...
She shared our sales enablement Notion database.
So there's lots of good content in there.
This deck will be in there, but there's like battle cards and other like sort of internal resources.
Some things you might want to look into from that as a resource.
And I'll try to add, continue to add more stuff.
I had like, there's a doc that I've been working on, on like programmatic SEO ideas on like, that's something I'd probably want to share with you guys as well.
Just, just, just to feed, feed the ideas.
And yeah, thanks for mentioning that database.
So I'm able to access the Dropbox files, but not the Google Doc files.
Ah, yes.
So some of those will need to be shareable with you guys.
Um, I will see what the best solution is for that, because.
Because.
Yeah, I don't want to add our team to, like, requests for every doc.
That would get unruly.
Yeah, yeah, that would get unruly.
We should, maybe that's something we, if there's a one-off or something, we could certainly share with your team.
Or if there's a way to, like, add, instead of making public to anyone, just, like, share with specific people on your team.
That might be good.
If there's, like, certain docs you want to share.
I don't know, Pippin, if you have any ideas here.
Yeah, think it's a case-by-case thing, because doc permissions are a little bit unruly at the moment.
Yeah, I'll take a look, and maybe we'll just point out a couple, if we can just get, like, a PDF version of those.
That'd be wonderful.
Yeah, if they just...
I think this deck specifically would be great.
Yeah, yeah, for sure.
If there's some high-priority items, just slack it to us, and we can spit out a PDF version or something.
Okay, wonderful.
All right.
Awesome.
Well, appreciate the time and the walkthrough.
It's super helpful, super in-depth.
So we will chat with you guys on Wednesday.
Have a great holiday weekend.
All right.
Sounds good.
Thanks so much, Erik.
Thank you, Bye-bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Deep dive into Deepgram's product offerings and capabilities for voice AI solutions](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Deepgram offers a comprehensive enterprise-ready voice AI platform with three core capabilities: speech-to-text, text-to-speech, and speech-to-speech](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=437.0)
  - [Their key differentiators include customizable models, industry-tuned solutions, and flexible deployment options (cloud, on-prem, dedicated)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=447.0)
  - [90% of current revenue is from speech-to-text, with text-to-speech and voice agent API being newer offerings launched in 2023](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
  - [Deepgram aims to compete on accuracy, latency, scalability, and cost-effectiveness for enterprise use cases](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=717.0)

## Topics

### Company Overview

  - [Deepgram is a foundational AI company focused on building voice models for voice AI](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=102.0)
  - [Research and engineering-led approach, productizing advanced models for enterprise use](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=131.0)
  - [Targeting untapped potential of voice data in enterprises (customer calls, sales calls, meetings)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)

### Speech-to-Text Capabilities

  - [Nova model family: Nova 2 (base model) and Nova 3 (premium, advanced model)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=948.0)
  - [Nova 3 key features:](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=948.0)
      - [Designed for noisy environments (e.g., call centers, drive-thrus)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1122.0)
      - [Enhanced numerical and industry-specific terminology recognition](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1154.0)
      - [Real-time multilingual support and PII redaction](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1043.0)
      - [Flexible self-service customization (100 terms at runtime)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1178.0)
  - [Industry-tuned and custom models available (e.g., healthcare, FSI)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=517.0)
  - [Expanding language support for Nova 3 (European languages added)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1255.0)

### Text-to-Speech Capabilities

  - [Competing with Eleven Labs, but focused on enterprise use cases rather than creative](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1335.0)
  - [40+ voices with localized accents and distinct voice profiles](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1447.0)
  - [Strengths in domain-specific accuracy, handling formatted terms](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1413.0)
  - [Cost-effective for high-volume, real-time enterprise scenarios](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1507.0)
  - [Multilingual support coming in Q4 2023](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1456.0)

### Speech-to-Speech (Voice Agent API)

  - [Unified API handling speech-to-text, LLM integration, and text-to-speech](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1588.0)
  - [Manages complex orchestration (turn-taking, interruptions, conversation logic)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1632.0)
  - [Features include prompt updating, voice switching, and function calling](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1753.0)
  - [Offers cloud, self-hosted, and dedicated deployment options](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1934.0)
  - [Launched for production use cases in June 2023](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1588.0)

### Enterprise Focus

  - [Tailored for mission-critical applications requiring high accuracy, low latency, scalability](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=851.0)
  - [Security and compliance certifications for regulated industries](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=675.0)
  - [Flexible deployment options (cloud, on-prem, dedicated instance)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=629.0)
  - [Partnerships with contact center and technology partners for turnkey solutions](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=713.0)

### Market Positioning

  - [90% of current revenue from speech-to-text solutions](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=729.0)
  - [Competing with cloud providers (AWS, Azure) and specialized players (Assembly AI, Rev.ai)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1135.0)
  - [Differentiation through customization, industry-tuned models, and enterprise-focused features](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=1089.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Erik to review Deepgram's sales enablement Notion database for additional resources](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=2259.0)
  - [Hasan to share document on programmatic SEO ideas](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=2241.0)
  - [Team to address Google Doc access issues for Erik (case-by-case basis or PDF versions)](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=2263.0)
  - [Follow-up meeting scheduled for Wednesday to continue strategy discussions](https://fathom.video/share/_GC4U3BbzonuMEWh2FsMDz3iyTcQbxz9?tab=summary&timestamp=2347.0)

Convert requested high-priority Google Docs to PDF, send to Erik; include presentation deck shown in meeting
